In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%matplotlib inline
In [2]:
performance = pd.read_csv('StudentPerformanceFactors.csv')

In [8]:
print("Exam_Score > 100:", (performance["Exam_Score"] > 100).sum())

Exam_Score > 100: 1

Observam ca maximul notei este 101, am verificat cate persoane au nota peste 100 sa vedem daca poate fi ceva general sau este un caz izolat/eroare de scriere cel mai probabil - am afisat linia studentului cu exam_score peste 100 pentru a verifica si celelalte statistici

In [9]:
performance.loc[performance["Exam_Score"] > 100, :]
Out[9]:
Hours_Studied Attendance Parental_Involvement Access_to_Resources Extracurricular_Activities Sleep_Hours Previous_Scores Motivation_Level Internet_Access Tutoring_Sessions Family_Income Teacher_Quality School_Type Peer_Influence Physical_Activity Learning_Disabilities Parental_Education_Level Distance_from_Home Gender Exam_Score
1525 27 98 Low Medium Yes 6 93 Low No 5 High High Public Positive 3 No High School Moderate Female 101
Avand in vedere ca overall are attenadance foarte ridicat si scoruri ridicate la multe dintre coloane, si avand in vedere ca valoarea s-ar modifica cu 1% dintr-un total de 6606 de persoane, o sa aleg sa-i modificam exam_score la 100 astfel neafectand setul de date mai deloc si consideram cazul doar ca o eroare de scriere
In [10]:
performance.loc[performance["Exam_Score"] == 101, "Exam_Score"] = 100
In [11]:
print("Max Exam_Score:", performance["Exam_Score"].max())

Max Exam_Score: 100
Verificam ca s-a produs schimbarea in tabel - totul e verificat acum
In [12]:
performance.isnull().sum()
Out[12]:

In [13]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3))
sns.heatmap(performance.isnull(), cbar=False, cmap="viridis")
plt.title("Missing values heatmap")
plt.show()
In [14]:
cols_with_missing = ["Teacher_Quality", "Parental_Education_Level", "Distance_from_Home"]

for col in cols_with_missing:
    tmp = performance.groupby(performance[col].isna())["Exam_Score"].mean()
    print(f"\nMean Exam_Score by {col} missing:")
    display(tmp)

Mean Exam_Score by Teacher_Quality missing:

Teacher_Quality
False    67.242610
True     66.641026
Name: Exam_Score, dtype: float64
Mean Exam_Score by Parental_Education_Level missing:

Parental_Education_Level
False    67.237993
True     67.055556
Name: Exam_Score, dtype: float64
Mean Exam_Score by Distance_from_Home missing:

Distance_from_Home
False    67.243731
True     66.432836
Name: Exam_Score, dtype: float64
Putem observa ca media notelor de la examen intre cei cu acele valori lipsa si cei ce le au completate este ~ egala, diferenta fiind foarte minora ~ diff < 1%, astfel putem considera ca nota de la examen nu depinde in mod direct de lipsa datelor respective, astfel voi alege sa completez cazurile lipsa cu valoarea cea mai frecventa pe acele coloane
In [15]:
cols_with_missing = ["Teacher_Quality", "Parental_Education_Level", "Distance_from_Home"]

for col in cols_with_missing:
    mode_val = performance[col].mode(dropna=True)[0]
    performance[col] = performance[col].fillna(mode_val)
In [16]:
performance[cols_with_missing].isna().sum()
Out[16]:
Teacher_Quality             0
Parental_Education_Level    0
Distance_from_Home          0
dtype: int64
In [17]:
performance.duplicated().sum()
Out[17]:
np.int64(0)

Am populat randurile lipsa cu valoarea medie, am verificat daca existe duplicate(nu avem), am verificat sa fim siguri ca nu mai exista valori nule in tabel si am salvat noile modificari( modiface outlier + modificari valori nule) intr-un nou tabel

In [18]:
performance.to_csv("performance_cleaned.csv", index=False)
In [19]:
performance2 = pd.read_csv('performance_cleaned.csv')
In [20]:
corr_matrix_performance = performance2.corr(numeric_only=True)
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix_performance, annot=True, cmap='Blues')
Out[20]:
<Axes: >

Putem observa ca cea mai puternica corelatie cu Exam_score este cu numarul orelor studiate si cu prezenta, iar abia pe locul 3 si 4 notele anterioare si sesiunele de meditatii dar cu un scor semnificativ mai scazut decat primele 2

In [21]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.regplot(data=performance2 , x="Attendance", y="Exam_Score", scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.25}, line_kws={"color": "red"})
plt.title("Attendance vs ExamScore")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.regplot(data=performance2, x="Hours_Studied", y="Exam_Score", scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.25}, line_kws={"color": "red"})
plt.title("Hours_Studied vs ExamScore")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [23]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
order = performance2.groupby("Motivation_Level")["Exam_Score"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).index
sns.boxplot(data=performance2, x="Motivation_Level", y="Exam_Score", order=order)
plt.title("ExamScore în funcție de MotivationLevel")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Motivation_Level-ul nu pare sa fie un factor major pentru Exam_Score, putem gasi valori mari in toate grupele(note foarte bune) + Putem observa ca media notelor este foarte apropiata indiferent de nivelul de motivare

In [24]:
lmh_map = {"Low": 0, "Medium": 1, "High": 2}

performance2["Parental_Involvement"]   = performance2["Parental_Involvement"].map(lmh_map)
performance2["Access_to_Resources"]    = performance2["Access_to_Resources"].map(lmh_map)
performance2["Motivation_Level"]       = performance2["Motivation_Level"].map(lmh_map)
performance2["Family_Income"]          = performance2["Family_Income"].map(lmh_map)
performance2["Teacher_Quality"]        = performance2["Teacher_Quality"].map(lmh_map)

yn_map = {"No": 0, "Yes": 1}
performance2["Extracurricular_Activities"] = performance2["Extracurricular_Activities"].map(yn_map)
performance2["Internet_Access"]            = performance2["Internet_Access"].map(yn_map)
performance2["Learning_Disabilities"]      = performance2["Learning_Disabilities"].map(yn_map)

school_map = {"Public": 0, "Private": 1}
performance2["School_Type"] = performance2["School_Type"].map(school_map)

gender_map = {"Male": 0, "Female": 1}
performance2["Gender"] = performance2["Gender"].map(gender_map)

peer_map = {"Negative": 0, "Neutral": 1, "Positive": 2}
performance2["Peer_Influence"] = performance2["Peer_Influence"].map(peer_map)

pedu_map = {"High School": 0, "College": 1, "Postgraduate": 2}
performance2["Parental_Education_Level"] = performance2["Parental_Education_Level"].map(pedu_map)

dist_map = {"Near": 0, "Moderate": 1, "Far": 2}
performance2["Distance_from_Home"] = performance2["Distance_from_Home"].map(dist_map)
In [25]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))  # sau (8, 6) pentru mai mult spațiu
corr_matrix_df = performance2.corr(numeric_only=True)

sns.heatmap(
    corr_matrix_df,
    annot=True,
    cmap='Blues',
    fmt=".2f",        # doar 2 zecimale
    annot_kws={"size": 8}  # font mai mic pentru numere
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Am transformat toate valorile non-numerice in valori numerice si am afisat matricea completa de corelatie, am mai gasit niste corelatii interesante, dar nu destul de semnificative din punct de vedere al corelatiei directe cu Exam_Score de ex: Access_to_Resource, Parental_Involvement si Peer_Influence au o corelatie relativ mica dar importanta de semnalizat, iar pe de alta parte, Gender-ul nu conteaza deloc si School_Type-ul la fel

In [26]:
performance2 = performance2.drop(columns=["Gender"], errors="ignore")
performance2.to_csv("performance_cleaned.csv", index=False)

Stergem coloana gender pentru ca am observat ca nu este deloc relevanta pentru Exam_Score

Impartim datele in seturi de antrenare, validare si testare

In [28]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
In [29]:
df = pd.read_csv("performance_cleaned.csv")
In [30]:
df.head()
Out[30]:
Hours_Studied Attendance Parental_Involvement Access_to_Resources Extracurricular_Activities Sleep_Hours Previous_Scores Motivation_Level Internet_Access Tutoring_Sessions Family_Income Teacher_Quality School_Type Peer_Influence Physical_Activity Learning_Disabilities Parental_Education_Level Distance_from_Home Exam_Score
0 23 84 0 2 0 7 73 0 1 0 0 1 0 2 3 0 0 0 67
1 19 64 0 1 0 8 59 0 1 2 1 1 0 0 4 0 1 1 61
2 24 98 1 1 1 7 91 1 1 2 1 1 0 1 4 0 2 0 74
3 29 89 0 1 1 8 98 1 1 1 1 1 0 0 4 0 0 1 71
4 19 92 1 1 1 6 65 1 1 3 1 2 0 1 4 0 1 0 70
In [31]:
features = df.drop("Exam_Score", axis=1)
labels = df["Exam_Score"]
In [32]:
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    features,      # X
    labels,        # y
    test_size=0.4, 
    random_state=42
)
In [33]:
X_test, X_val, y_test, y_val = train_test_split(X_test, y_test, test_size=0.5, random_state=42)
In [34]:
X_train

Salvam datele in fisiere CSV noi

In [43]:
X_train.to_csv('train_features.csv', index=False)
X_val.to_csv('val_features.csv', index=False)
X_test.to_csv('test_features.csv', index=False)

y_train.to_csv('train_labels.csv', index=False)
y_val.to_csv('val_labels.csv', index=False)
y_test.to_csv('test_labels.csv', index=False)
In [44]:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor    # modelul ML pe care-l vom folosi
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score    # avem nevoie pentru evaluarea cross-validation
In [45]:
train_features = pd.read_csv('train_features.csv')
train_labels = pd.read_csv('train_labels.csv')
In [46]:
rf_model = RandomForestRegressor()

rf_model_scores = cross_val_score(
    rf_model,
    features,
    labels.values.ravel(),
    cv=5,
    scoring="neg_mean_absolute_error"
)
In [47]:
rf_model_scores
Out[47]:
array([-1.14234493, -1.13985628, -1.1193944 , -1.06651022, -1.03400454])

Modelul greseste cu circa un punct la nota examenului

In [48]:
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
In [49]:
rf_model = RandomForestRegressor()

h_params = {
    "n_estimators": [50, 100, 200],
    "max_depth": [5, 10, 20, None]
}

cv_rf_model = GridSearchCV(
    rf_model,
    h_params,
    cv=5,
    scoring="neg_mean_absolute_error"
)

cv_rf_model.fit(features, labels.values.ravel())
Out[49]:
GridSearchCV(cv=5, estimator=RandomForestRegressor(),
             param_grid={'max_depth': [5, 10, 20, None],
                         'n_estimators': [50, 100, 200]},
             scoring='neg_mean_absolute_error')
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
estimator estimator: estimator object

This is assumed to implement the scikit-learn estimator interface.
Either estimator needs to provide a ``score`` function,
or ``scoring`` must be passed. 		RandomForestRegressor()
param_grid param_grid: dict or list of dictionaries

Dictionary with parameters names (`str`) as keys and lists of
parameter settings to try as values, or a list of such
dictionaries, in which case the grids spanned by each dictionary
in the list are explored. This enables searching over any sequence
of parameter settings. 		{'max_depth': [5, 10, ...], 'n_estimators': [50, 100, ...]}
scoring scoring: str, callable, list, tuple or dict, default=None

Strategy to evaluate the performance of the cross-validated model on
the test set.

If `scoring` represents a single score, one can use:

- a single string (see :ref:`scoring_string_names`);
- a callable (see :ref:`scoring_callable`) that returns a single value;
- `None`, the `estimator`'s
:ref:`default evaluation criterion ` is used.

If `scoring` represents multiple scores, one can use:

- a list or tuple of unique strings;
- a callable returning a dictionary where the keys are the metric
names and the values are the metric scores;
- a dictionary with metric names as keys and callables as values.

See :ref:`multimetric_grid_search` for an example. 		'neg_mean_absolute_error'
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

Number of jobs to run in parallel.
``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary `
for more details.

.. versionchanged:: v0.20
`n_jobs` default changed from 1 to None 		None
refit refit: bool, str, or callable, default=True

Refit an estimator using the best found parameters on the whole
dataset.

For multiple metric evaluation, this needs to be a `str` denoting the
scorer that would be used to find the best parameters for refitting
the estimator at the end.

Where there are considerations other than maximum score in
choosing a best estimator, ``refit`` can be set to a function which
returns the selected ``best_index_`` given ``cv_results_``. In that
case, the ``best_estimator_`` and ``best_params_`` will be set
according to the returned ``best_index_`` while the ``best_score_``
attribute will not be available.

The refitted estimator is made available at the ``best_estimator_``
attribute and permits using ``predict`` directly on this
``GridSearchCV`` instance.

Also for multiple metric evaluation, the attributes ``best_index_``,
``best_score_`` and ``best_params_`` will only be available if
``refit`` is set and all of them will be determined w.r.t this specific
scorer.

See ``scoring`` parameter to know more about multiple metric
evaluation.

See :ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_model_selection_plot_grid_search_digits.py`
to see how to design a custom selection strategy using a callable
via `refit`.

See :ref:`this example
`
for an example of how to use ``refit=callable`` to balance model
complexity and cross-validated score.

.. versionchanged:: 0.20
Support for callable added. 		True
cv cv: int, cross-validation generator or an iterable, default=None

Determines the cross-validation splitting strategy.
Possible inputs for cv are:

- None, to use the default 5-fold cross validation,
- integer, to specify the number of folds in a `(Stratified)KFold`,
- :term:`CV splitter`,
- An iterable yielding (train, test) splits as arrays of indices.

For integer/None inputs, if the estimator is a classifier and ``y`` is
either binary or multiclass, :class:`StratifiedKFold` is used. In all
other cases, :class:`KFold` is used. These splitters are instantiated
with `shuffle=False` so the splits will be the same across calls.

Refer :ref:`User Guide ` for the various
cross-validation strategies that can be used here.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
``cv`` default value if None changed from 3-fold to 5-fold. 		5
verbose verbose: int

Controls the verbosity: the higher, the more messages.

- >1 : the computation time for each fold and parameter candidate is
displayed;
- >2 : the score is also displayed;
- >3 : the fold and candidate parameter indexes are also displayed
together with the starting time of the computation. 		0
pre_dispatch pre_dispatch: int, or str, default='2*n_jobs'

Controls the number of jobs that get dispatched during parallel
execution. Reducing this number can be useful to avoid an
explosion of memory consumption when more jobs get dispatched
than CPUs can process. This parameter can be:

- None, in which case all the jobs are immediately created and spawned. Use
this for lightweight and fast-running jobs, to avoid delays due to on-demand
spawning of the jobs
- An int, giving the exact number of total jobs that are spawned
- A str, giving an expression as a function of n_jobs, as in '2*n_jobs' 		'2*n_jobs'
error_score error_score: 'raise' or numeric, default=np.nan

Value to assign to the score if an error occurs in estimator fitting.
If set to 'raise', the error is raised. If a numeric value is given,
FitFailedWarning is raised. This parameter does not affect the refit
step, which will always raise the error. 		nan
return_train_score return_train_score: bool, default=False

If ``False``, the ``cv_results_`` attribute will not include training
scores.
Computing training scores is used to get insights on how different
parameter settings impact the overfitting/underfitting trade-off.
However computing the scores on the training set can be computationally
expensive and is not strictly required to select the parameters that
yield the best generalization performance.

.. versionadded:: 0.19

.. versionchanged:: 0.21
Default value was changed from ``True`` to ``False`` 		False 
RandomForestRegressor(max_depth=20, n_estimators=200)
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"squared_error", "absolute_error", "friedman_mse", "poisson"}, default="squared_error"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria
are "squared_error" for the mean squared error, which is equal to
variance reduction as feature selection criterion and minimizes the L2
loss using the mean of each terminal node, "friedman_mse", which uses
mean squared error with Friedman's improvement score for potential
splits, "absolute_error" for the mean absolute error, which minimizes
the L1 loss using the median of each terminal node, and "poisson" which
uses reduction in Poisson deviance to find splits.
Training using "absolute_error" is significantly slower
than when using "squared_error".

.. versionadded:: 0.18
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) criterion.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
Poisson criterion. 		'squared_error'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		20
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default=1.0

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None or 1.0, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::
The default of 1.0 is equivalent to bagged trees and more
randomness can be achieved by setting smaller values, e.g. 0.3.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to 1.0.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		1.0
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		None
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [50]:
cv_rf_model.best_params_
Out[50]:
{'max_depth': 20, 'n_estimators': 200}

cv_rf_model.cv_results_

 'std_fit_time': array([0.03162022, 0.04615833, 0.28017428, 0.12365157, 0.12885368,

 'mean_score_time': array([0.01111455, 0.02082686, 0.03809233, 0.01361351, 0.02795839,

 'std_score_time': array([0.0016574 , 0.00499797, 0.00963432, 0.00336486, 0.00482201,

 'param_max_depth': masked_array(data=[5, 5, 5, 10, 10, 10, 20, 20, 20, None, None, None],

 'param_n_estimators': masked_array(data=[50, 100, 200, 50, 100, 200, 50, 100, 200, 50, 100, 200],
              mask=[False, False, False, False, False, False, False, False,
                    False, False, False, False],
        fill_value=999999),

 'split1_test_score': array([-1.5330554 , -1.52925406, -1.52303078, -1.23121045, -1.22009265,

        -1.12527631, -1.11242998]),
 'split3_test_score': array([-1.45968173, -1.4605809 , -1.45538533, -1.1636376 , -1.15859318,

 'split4_test_score': array([-1.41813296, -1.41221773, -1.40692519, -1.14097395, -1.12965306,

 'mean_test_score': array([-1.487308  , -1.48423852, -1.4809028 , -1.19946467, -1.18572722,

 'std_test_score': array([0.04325329, 0.04346032, 0.04428859, 0.03991485, 0.03662923,

In [52]:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score
In [53]:
train_features = pd.read_csv('train_features.csv')
train_labels = pd.read_csv('train_labels.csv')

val_features = pd.read_csv('val_features.csv')
val_labels = pd.read_csv('val_labels.csv')

test_features = pd.read_csv('test_features.csv')
test_labels = pd.read_csv('test_labels.csv')
In [54]:
rf_model_1 = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=None, random_state=42)
rf_model_1.fit(train_features, train_labels.values.ravel())
Out[54]:
RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"squared_error", "absolute_error", "friedman_mse", "poisson"}, default="squared_error"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria
are "squared_error" for the mean squared error, which is equal to
variance reduction as feature selection criterion and minimizes the L2
loss using the mean of each terminal node, "friedman_mse", which uses
mean squared error with Friedman's improvement score for potential
splits, "absolute_error" for the mean absolute error, which minimizes
the L1 loss using the median of each terminal node, and "poisson" which
uses reduction in Poisson deviance to find splits.
Training using "absolute_error" is significantly slower
than when using "squared_error".

.. versionadded:: 0.18
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) criterion.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
Poisson criterion. 		'squared_error'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		None
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default=1.0

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None or 1.0, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::
The default of 1.0 is equivalent to bagged trees and more
randomness can be achieved by setting smaller values, e.g. 0.3.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to 1.0.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		1.0
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [55]:
rf_model_2 = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=20, random_state=42)
rf_model_2.fit(train_features, train_labels.values.ravel())
Out[55]:
RandomForestRegressor(max_depth=20, n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"squared_error", "absolute_error", "friedman_mse", "poisson"}, default="squared_error"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria
are "squared_error" for the mean squared error, which is equal to
variance reduction as feature selection criterion and minimizes the L2
loss using the mean of each terminal node, "friedman_mse", which uses
mean squared error with Friedman's improvement score for potential
splits, "absolute_error" for the mean absolute error, which minimizes
the L1 loss using the median of each terminal node, and "poisson" which
uses reduction in Poisson deviance to find splits.
Training using "absolute_error" is significantly slower
than when using "squared_error".

.. versionadded:: 0.18
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) criterion.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
Poisson criterion. 		'squared_error'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		20
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default=1.0

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None or 1.0, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::
The default of 1.0 is equivalent to bagged trees and more
randomness can be achieved by setting smaller values, e.g. 0.3.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to 1.0.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		1.0
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [56]:
rf_model_3 = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, max_depth=None, random_state=42)
rf_model_3.fit(train_features, train_labels.values.ravel())
Out[56]:
RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		100
criterion criterion: {"squared_error", "absolute_error", "friedman_mse", "poisson"}, default="squared_error"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria
are "squared_error" for the mean squared error, which is equal to
variance reduction as feature selection criterion and minimizes the L2
loss using the mean of each terminal node, "friedman_mse", which uses
mean squared error with Friedman's improvement score for potential
splits, "absolute_error" for the mean absolute error, which minimizes
the L1 loss using the median of each terminal node, and "poisson" which
uses reduction in Poisson deviance to find splits.
Training using "absolute_error" is significantly slower
than when using "squared_error".

.. versionadded:: 0.18
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) criterion.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
Poisson criterion. 		'squared_error'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		None
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default=1.0

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None or 1.0, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::
The default of 1.0 is equivalent to bagged trees and more
randomness can be achieved by setting smaller values, e.g. 0.3.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to 1.0.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		1.0
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None

In [60]:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score
import numpy as np

for model in [rf_model_1, rf_model_2, rf_model_3]:
    y_pred = model.predict(val_features)

    mae  = round(mean_absolute_error(val_labels, y_pred), 3)
    mse  = mean_squared_error(val_labels, y_pred)      # doar MSE
    rmse = round(np.sqrt(mse), 3)                      # radical din MSE
    r2   = round(r2_score(val_labels, y_pred), 3)

    print(
        f'max_depth: {model.max_depth}, n_estimators: {model.n_estimators} '
        f'__ MAE: {mae} / RMSE: {rmse} / R2: {r2}'
    )

max_depth: None, n_estimators: 200 __ MAE: 1.131 / RMSE: 2.27 / R2: 0.641
max_depth: 20, n_estimators: 200 __ MAE: 1.131 / RMSE: 2.268 / R2: 0.642
max_depth: None, n_estimators: 100 __ MAE: 1.142 / RMSE: 2.289 / R2: 0.635
In [61]:
best_model = cv_rf_model.best_estimator_
In [62]:
print(cv_rf_model.best_params_)

{'max_depth': 20, 'n_estimators': 200}
In [63]:
y_pred_test = best_model.predict(test_features)

mae  = round(mean_absolute_error(test_labels, y_pred_test), 3)
mse  = mean_squared_error(test_labels, y_pred_test)
rmse = round(np.sqrt(mse), 3)
r2   = round(r2_score(test_labels, y_pred_test), 3)

print(
    f'Model final (GridSearchCV) --> \n\n'
    f' max_depth: {best_model.max_depth}, \n'
    f' n_estimators: {best_model.n_estimators}, \n\n'
    f' MAE: {mae} \n'
    f' RMSE: {rmse} \n'
    f' R2: {r2}'
)

Model final (GridSearchCV) --> 

 max_depth: 20, 
 n_estimators: 200, 

 MAE: 0.375 
 RMSE: 0.749 
 R2: 0.959

0 23 84 0 2 0 7 73 0 1 0 0 1 0 2 3 0 0 0 67

In [66]:
features = df.drop('Exam_Score', axis=1)
labels = df['Exam_Score']
In [67]:
best_model = cv_rf_model.best_estimator_

best_model.fit(features, labels)

y_pred_all = best_model.predict(features)

mae_all  = round(mean_absolute_error(labels, y_pred_all), 3)
mse_all  = mean_squared_error(labels, y_pred_all)
rmse_all = round(np.sqrt(mse_all), 3)
r2_all   = round(r2_score(labels, y_pred_all), 3)

print(
    f'Model (pe tot setul) --> \n\n'
    f' max_depth: {best_model.max_depth}, \n'
    f' n_estimators: {best_model.n_estimators}, \n\n'
    f' MAE: {mae_all} \n'
    f' RMSE: {rmse_all} \n'
    f' R2: {r2_all}'
)

Model (pe tot setul) --> 

 max_depth: 20, 
 n_estimators: 200, 

 MAE: 0.407 
 RMSE: 0.854 
 R2: 0.952

In [82]:
student = {
    "Hours_Studied": 16,
    "Attendance": 88,
    "Parental_Involvement": 2,
    "Access_to_Resources": 1,
    "Extracurricular_Activities": 1,
    "Sleep_Hours": 7,
    "Previous_Scores": 70,
    "Motivation_Level": 1,
    "Internet_Access": 1,
    "Tutoring_Sessions": 2,
    "Family_Income": 0,
    "Teacher_Quality": 2,
    "School_Type": 0,
    "Peer_Influence": 2,
    "Physical_Activity": 4,
    "Learning_Disabilities": 0,
    "Parental_Education_Level": 1,
    "Distance_from_Home": 0,
    "Gender": 0,
}


df_student = pd.DataFrame([student])[features.columns]

pred_score = best_model.predict(df_student)[0]

print(f"Scor prezis: {pred_score:.2f}")

Scor prezis: 68.81
Pe baza datelor introduse pentru noul student, modelul aproximeaza exam_score-ul de 68.84
In [83]:
def predict_for(stud_dict):
    df = pd.DataFrame([stud_dict])[features.columns]
    return best_model.predict(df)[0]

base_pred = predict_for(student)

scenarii = []

#S1: Attendance de la 88 la 98
s1 = student.copy()
s1["Attendance"] = 98
scenarii.append(("Crestere prezenta la 98%", predict_for(s1)))

#S2: +4 ore de studiu (16 -> 26)
s2 = student.copy()
s2["Hours_Studied"] = 26
scenarii.append(("+10 ore de studiu", predict_for(s2)))

#S3: +1 sesiune de meditatii (1 -> 3)
s3 = student.copy()
s3["Tutoring_Sessions"] = 3
scenarii.append(("+2 sesiune de meditatii", predict_for(s3)))

#S4: combinat (Attendance 98, Hours_Studied 26, Tutoring 3)
s4 = student.copy()
s4["Attendance"] = 98
s4["Hours_Studied"] = 26
s4["Tutoring_Sessions"] = 3
scenarii.append(("Toate cele 3 schimbari", predict_for(s4)))

print(f"Scor initial prezis: {base_pred:.2f}")
for descr, pred in scenarii:
    delta = pred - base_pred
    print(f"{descr}: {pred:.2f}  (delta = {delta:+.2f} puncte)")

Scor initial prezis: 68.81
Crestere prezenta la 98%: 70.36  (delta = +1.55 puncte)
+10 ore de studiu: 70.77  (delta = +1.96 puncte)
+2 sesiune de meditatii: 69.42  (delta = +0.61 puncte)
Toate cele 3 schimbari: 73.24  (delta = +4.43 puncte)

Am adaugat 4 exemple de studenti noi in care am marit treptat urmatoarele: pentru primele doar Attendance cu 10% din care a rezultat un score mai bun cu 1.55

Pentru al doilea student am marit orele de studiu de la 16 la 26 si am obtinut un score mai bun cu 1.96

Pentru al treilea student am marit orele de meditatii cu 2 sesiune si am obtinut un rezultat mai bun cu 0.61

Iar pentru ultimul caz am marit cele 3 categorii simultan si a rezultat un rezultat mai bun cu 4.43

Concluzii finale

Am inceput proiectul prin afisarea data set-ului pentru a-mi crea o perspectiva de asamblu asupra lui. Am verificat data type-urile coloanelor, iar apoi am verificat limitele sa vedem daca gasim nereguli(ex. outliers). Apoi am verificat valorile nule sa vedem daca exista si daca influenteaza in mod semnificativ exam_score-ul. Apoi am creat matricea de corelatie sa vedem ce categorii/coloane influenteaza cel mai mult in mod direct nota de la examen. Am transformat valorile literare in valori numerice pentru o imagine mai ampla, dupa care am imparitit setul de date in 3 seturi pentru antrenare, validare si testare urmand principiul 60-20-20. Am aflat si selectat modelul optim(primele 3 cele mai optime), am antrenat modelul final pe intregul set de antrenare, iar dupa am folosit modelul pe un student nou pentru a aproxima nota examenului si am testat inca 4 simulari pe 4 studenti in caz de marim valorile celor mai importante categorii in corelatie directa cu Exam_Score.

In [ ]:
